planting.note: Direct sow in moist to wet, acidic to neutral soil at wetland edges and in rain gardens; hop sedge grows naturally in shallow standing water, ditches, swamp margins, and wet bottomlands across eastern North America. Division in spring is equally effective.

directions: Plant in moist to wet or seasonally flooded, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; hop sedge is one of the most distinctive native wetland sedges of eastern North America, recognized by its unusually large, rounded seed heads that strikingly resemble small hop (Humulus) cones — each head is a dense cluster of inflated, spine-tipped perigynia that give the fruiting structure a spiky, armored appearance unlike any other native sedge. The plant grows naturally in shallow marshes, forested swamp edges, stream banks, and periodically flooded bottomlands from Quebec to Texas. In rain gardens and bioswales, hop sedge tolerates both seasonal inundation and brief dry periods once established. The large seed heads are consumed by wood ducks, mallards, and marsh birds. An excellent structural element for wet-site plantings where its distinctive fruiting heads add bold textural interest from summer through fall.

planting.note: Direct sow in wet, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; fringed sedge grows naturally along shaded stream banks and forested seeps and is one of the few native sedges adapted to deep woodland shade with standing water or continuously saturated soil.

directions: Plant in wet to consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade along stream banks, forested swamp edges, and shaded seeps; fringed sedge is one of the most shade-tolerant of the large native wetland sedges, growing naturally in the deep shade of forested bottomlands and streamside forests across eastern North America. Its most distinctive feature is the long, pendant female spikes — drooping catkins that hang loosely from arching stems, the scales tapering to delicate bristle tips (the 'fringe') — which create a graceful, weeping texture in the woodland garden. The clumps form slowly expanding, elegant tufts of medium-textured, grass-like foliage that remain attractive throughout the growing season. Excellent in rain gardens with overhead tree cover, shaded bioswales, and streamside restoration where full-sun sedges fail due to shade. The seeds are consumed by various marsh and woodland birds.

planting.note: Direct sow in moist to wet, acidic to neutral soil in sun to partial shade; lurid sedge is among the most adaptable native wetland sedges — it tolerates both seasonal flooding and brief drying, making it excellent for rain gardens and wet-site landscaping.

directions: Plant in moist to seasonally wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; lurid sedge is a widely distributed native sedge of streambanks, ditches, wet meadows, and pond margins throughout eastern North America. The distinctive, densely packed, cylindrical to oval female flower heads — pale green when young, becoming dark and almost blackish at maturity — give it a bold, architectural appearance in the rain garden or bioswale. The species name refers to the dark, somewhat somber coloring of the mature fruiting heads. Lurid sedge is highly adaptable: it tolerates temporary inundation, moderate drought once established, clay or sandy soils, and pH ranging from 5.5 to 7.5 — an unusual combination that makes it one of the most versatile natives for challenging wet sites. Excellent for stream bank stabilization and rain garden margins where it spreads by short rhizomes to form erosion-controlling colonies.

planting.note: Direct sow in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; squarrose sedge grows naturally in forested floodplains and shaded swamp margins. Plants grow slowly from seed but divide readily in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun in forested wetland gardens and shaded rain gardens; squarrose sedge is a native sedge of swamp forests, forested floodplains, and shaded stream banks across the eastern United States, distinguished from related species by its unique fruiting heads in which the perigynia (seed cases) spread outward at nearly right angles to the stem axis — giving the spike a distinctly square-shouldered, cylindrical appearance. The reflexed perigynia tip creates a rough, bristly texture on the seed heads that provides excellent habitat texture in woodland plantings. Tolerates partial shade better than many wetland sedges and is excellent in rain garden plantings with overhead shade from existing trees. The seeds are consumed by various wetland birds. Forms tidy, non-aggressive clumps that stay in scale for residential rain garden plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Frank's sedge is particularly well adapted to the heavy clay alluvial soils of floodplains and is a reliable plant for difficult wet clay sites in the Southeast.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, heavy clay to loamy soils in partial shade to full sun in floodplains, swamp edges, and wet gardens; Frank's sedge is a native wetland sedge of the lower Midwest and Southeast, growing naturally in alluvial floodplain forests, swamp margins, and wet meadows from Pennsylvania south to Florida and west to Missouri and Kansas. It is particularly adapted to the heavy, periodically flooded clay soils typical of river bottomlands in the Southeast — a substrate that challenges most other native sedges. The barrel-shaped, spiky seed heads with spreading perigynia resemble those of Carex squarrosa but tend to be more numerous and loosely spaced on the stem. An excellent choice for rain garden plantings in clay soils and for stream bank restoration in the Gulf South. The robust clumps provide winter cover for small birds and the seeds are consumed by waterfowl.
